title: Memory Tricks, Mnemonics & Tips — Self Introduction (Day 2)
description: Creative memory tricks, mnemonics, and tips to never forget the key phrases of Self Introduction.

sections.title: The PERFECT Introduction Formula — Mnemonic: PEFHGL
sections.description: Remember the 6 parts of a perfect introduction using the word 'PEFHGL' — think of it as 'People Enjoy Friendly, Honest, Goal-oriented Learners'

breakdown.example: My name is Rahul. I am 25. I am from Delhi.
breakdown.trick: P = Personal — ye cheez sabse personal hai

breakdown.example: I completed my B.Tech from DTU in 2022 with 8.5 CGPA.
I work as a developer at TCS. I have been there for 2 years.
I love reading books and playing cricket.
breakdown.trick: H = Hobbies — aap kya karte hain free time mein

breakdown.example: My goal is to become a senior developer in 3 years.
breakdown.trick: G = Goals — aap kahan jaana chahte hain

breakdown.example: I look forward to working with your team.
breakdown.trick: L = Looking Forward — professionally close karein

sections.quickTest: Can you say all 6 parts without looking? Try now!

sections.title: Never Say 'Myself Rahul' — The MNIFC Rule
sections.description: Remember what NOT to say and WHAT TO SAY instead.

tricks.trick: Think of 'MY' in 'My name is' — MY belongs to ME. Myself belongs to reflection, not introduction.
tricks.memory_image: Imagine looking in a mirror and saying 'Myself Rahul' — mirrors are for 'myself'. When meeting people, face them and say 'My name is Rahul'.
tricks.hindi_trick: Aaina = myself. Log = My name is. Jab log milte hain, mirror ki zaroorat nahi!

tricks.trick: In English, names are not 'good' or 'bad'. Only in Hindi do we say 'aapka shubh naam'. In English, a name is just a name.
tricks.memory_image: Imagine 'good name' has an X through it in English — the word 'good' is not needed before 'name' in English.
tricks.hindi_trick: 'Shubh naam' = Hindi concept. English mein naam = naam. Simple.

tricks.trick: Birth happened in the PAST — use PAST tense. You cannot be 'being born' right now! Use 'I WAS born'.
tricks.memory_image: Think of a baby being born. That happened YEARS ago. Past event = past tense. 'I WAS born'.
tricks.hindi_trick: Janm = past mein hua tha. Past event = WAS. 'I WAS born in Delhi.'

sections.title: FOR vs SINCE — Never Confuse Again
sections.hindiTitle: FOR vs SINCE — कभी Confuse मत होना
sections.description: The most common tense mistake in introductions — using FOR vs SINCE incorrectly.
sections.mainRule: FOR + DURATION (period of time) | SINCE + POINT IN TIME (specific moment)

tricks.trick: FOR has 4 letters. FOUR letters = a NUMBER (duration). Think: FOR = 4 = number = duration.
examples: for 3 years
for 6 months
for a week
for a long time
tricks.memory_sentence: I have been studying FOR three years.

tricks.trick: SINCE = Since a SPECIFIC moment. Both SINCE and SPECIFIC start with 'S'!
examples: since 2020
since January
since last Monday
since I was 10

tricks.trick: Can you say 'the year/date'? Then use SINCE. Can you say 'a period'? Use FOR.
examples: I have been here ___ 3 years. (FOR — 3 years is a period)
I have been here ___ 2021. (SINCE — 2021 is a point)
I have been here ___ last Monday. (SINCE — last Monday is a point)
I have been here ___ a long time. (FOR — a long time is a period)

sections.title: A vs AN — The Sound Rule (Not Spelling Rule)
sections.hindiTitle: A vs AN — आवाज़ का नियम (Spelling का नहीं)
sections.description: The most misunderstood grammar rule in India — a/an is about SOUND not SPELLING.
sections.mainRule: Use A before consonant SOUNDS. Use AN before vowel SOUNDS. Sound = pronunciation, NOT spelling.

tricks.trick: Say the word aloud. Does your mouth open with a vowel sound (A, E, I, O, U sound)? Then AN. Consonant sound? Then A.
tricks.hindi_trick: A/AN ka rule spelling pe nahi, bolne ki awaaz pe hai!

tricks.trick: A/AN before professions — memorize these common ones:

sections.title: Stative Verbs — Never Use -ING With These
sections.hindiTitle: Stative Verbs — इनके साथ -ING कभी नहीं
sections.description: The most common grammar error in India — using continuous tense with stative verbs.
sections.mainRule: Stative verbs describe STATES (not actions). States don't have -ING form.

tricks.trick: Memorize: LIKES KNOW HAVE — these three never use -ING for states.
tricks.mnemonic: LKH = Like, Know, Have — these three stative verbs are very common mistakes.

wrong_right.wrong: I am wanting to work here

tricks.trick: Ask: Is this something you can 'do' right now and stop? Action → can use -ING. Is this a state of being? State → NO -ING.
examples: Running — you can start and stop running. Action → I am running. ✓
Loving — you cannot 'start and stop' loving. State → I love (not I am loving). ✗
Knowing — you cannot 'do knowing'. State → I know (not I am knowing). ✗
Working — you can start and stop working. Action → I am working. ✓

sections.title: Preposition Memory Tricks — AT/IN/FOR/FROM
sections.description: Remember which preposition to use in introductions.

tricks.concept: Work AT/FOR companies | Work IN industries
tricks.trick: Think of a dartboard: you aim AT a specific target (company). You work IN a big area (industry/sector).

examples.example: I work AT Google / I work FOR Google
I work IN the IT sector / I work IN finance

tricks.hindi_trick: Company ka naam = AT/FOR. Industry/sector = IN.

tricks.concept: I am FROM [city] | I live IN [city]
tricks.trick: FROM = origin (where you came from, past). IN = current location (where you are now, present).
examples: I am FROM Delhi (but I don't live there now)
I live IN Mumbai (where I am right now)
I am FROM Delhi but I currently live IN Mumbai.
tricks.hindi_trick: FROM = aap kahan se aaye (origin). IN = aap abhi kahan hain (current).

tricks.concept: I GRADUATED FROM | I COMPLETED my degree FROM
tricks.trick: FROM = source. Education comes FROM a college. So you graduate/complete FROM a college.
examples: I graduated FROM IIT Delhi. ✓
I completed my degree FROM IIT Delhi. ✓
I studied AT IIT Delhi. ✓ (AT = the place)
I studied IN IIT Delhi. ✗ (IN is wrong here)

tricks.concept: Interested IN | Fond OF | Good AT
examples: Interested IN music (I = interested, IN = the preposition after)
Fond OF reading (F = fond, OF = the preposition after)
Good AT cricket (G = good, AT = the preposition after)
Passionate ABOUT technology (P = passionate, ABOUT)
Excited ABOUT the role (ABOUT for excitement)

sections.title: Template-Based Memory — 6 Ready Templates
sections.description: Memorize these 6 templates and you can introduce yourself in any situation.

templates.template: Good [time]. My name is [Name]. I am from [City]. I completed my [Degree] from [College] in [Year] with [Score]. I have been working as [Role] at [Company] for [Duration]. My key strength is [Strength]. I am passionate about [Field]. My goal is to [Goal]. Thank you for this opportunity.
templates.trick: Memorize as 9 slots: Greeting → Name → City → Education → Job → Strength → Passion → Goal → Thanks

templates.template: Hi everyone! My name is [Name]. My friends call me [Nickname]. I am from [City]. I completed 12th with [%] in [Stream]. I love [Hobby1], [Hobby2], and [Hobby3]. My dream is to become [Career]. Nice to meet you all!
templates.trick: Memorize as: Name → Nickname → City → 12th → 3 Hobbies → Dream → Greeting

templates.template: Good [time] everyone! My name is [Name]. I am joining as [Role]. I have [X] years of experience in [Field]. In my free time, I enjoy [Hobby]. I look forward to working with all of you!
templates.trick: Short and professional: Name → Role → Experience → Hobby → Looking Forward

templates.template: Hello everyone! I am [Name], [Role] at [Company], joining from [City]. I have been in [Field] for [Duration]. Excited to be on this call! Let's get started.
templates.trick: For online meetings: brief and action-oriented. Name → Role → Company → City → Experience → Excitement

templates.template: Hi all! I am [Name] from [City]. I [work as/am studying]. I joined this group because [reason]. Looking forward to learning with everyone! 😊
templates.trick: Casual: Name → City → What you do → Why you joined → Positive close

templates.template: Good [time]! Am I speaking with [Name]? This is [Your Name] calling from [Company]. I am [Role]. The reason for my call is [purpose]. Do you have a few minutes?
templates.trick: Phone: Confirm receiver → Identify yourself → Company → Role → Purpose → Ask for time

sections.title: Word Association Tricks — Remember Key Vocabulary
sections.hindiTitle: Word Association Tricks — Vocabulary याद रखने के तरीके
sections.description: Creative word associations to remember important vocabulary for self-introduction.

tricks.association: Imagine a fire 🔥 inside your chest. Passion = that fire. When you are PASSIONATE about something, it burns like a fire inside you.
tricks.sentence: I am passionate about English learning.

tricks.meaning: to be an expert in one area
tricks.association: SPECIAL + ize. When you SPECIALIZE, you become SPECIAL in one area. A specialist is someone special in their field.
Think EN-THUSI-ASTIC. 'Thusi' sounds like 'khushi' in Hindi. Being enthusiastic = being khushi (happy) about something!
tricks.sentence: I am enthusiastic about this opportunity.

tricks.meaning: able to do many things well
tricks.association: Verse-atile. A verse can be in many forms (poem, song, rap). Versatile = capable in many forms.
tricks.sentence: I am a versatile professional with skills in marketing, design, and content.

tricks.association: PROFI-CIENT sounds like 'profit' + 'efficient'. A proficient person is efficient AND profitable in their skill.
tricks.sentence: I am proficient in Python and SQL.

tricks.association: CO + LABOR + ATE. CO = together, LABOR = work. To collaborate = to work together.
tricks.sentence: I enjoy collaborating with cross-functional teams.

tricks.association: Think of rubber. Rubber BOUNCES BACK when you press it. A resilient person bounces back from problems — just like rubber!
tricks.sentence: I am resilient and can perform well under pressure.

tricks.association: DILI = Delhi (capital of India, very busy and hardworking). GENT = gentleman. A diligent person is a hardworking gentleman!
tricks.sentence: I am a diligent worker who always meets deadlines.

sections.title: Rhythm and Rhyme Tricks
sections.description: Catchy rhymes and rhythms to remember grammar rules.

tricks.rule: Use PAST tense for completed education
tricks.rhyme: Yesterday I learned, today I earn — use PAST tense when the study is done!
tricks.application: I COMPLETED (past) my B.Tech in 2022. Now I WORK (present).

tricks.rhyme: AT a point, IN a space, FROM the origin — remember your place!
tricks.application: Work AT Google | Live IN Delhi | Come FROM Patna

tricks.rhyme: LOVE, LIKE, and KNOW — they do not FLOW with -ING, just so!
tricks.application: I LIKE this company (not 'I am liking') | I KNOW the answer (not 'I am knowing')

tricks.rhyme: FOR a period, SINCE a date — know the difference, don't be late!
tricks.application: I have worked FOR 3 years | I have worked SINCE 2021

sections.title: Visualization Techniques — See It, Remember It
sections.description: Create mental images to remember grammar rules permanently.

tricks.visualization: Imagine yourself at a party. You walk up to someone with a name tag that says 'My name is [Name]'. You point at the name tag and say 'My name is'. You don't say 'Myself' — that's not on the name tag!
See a sepia photograph of yourself as a newborn baby. Old photos = past. 'I WAS born' — same old photo energy, same past tense.

tricks.rule: I have BEEN working FOR 3 years (not 'I am working since 3 years')
tricks.visualization: Imagine a timeline. There's a dot for '3 years ago' (when you started). There's a dot for 'today'. A line connects them. The line = 'have been'. The total length = FOR 3 years.
tricks.anchor: TIMELINE LINE = have been | LENGTH = for [duration]

tricks.visualization: Imagine an open mouth (O shape) before vowel sounds. When the sound opens your mouth (A, E, I, O, U sounds) — use AN.

sections.title: Daily Practice Memory Tricks
sections.description: Habits and practices to make self-introduction second nature.

habits.description: Har subah mirror ke saamne apna introduction do. Pehle hafte: formal template. Doosre hafte: apne words mein. Teesre hafte: bina soche seedha bolo.
habits.goal: 30 days mein — no thinking required. Just speak!

habits.description: Phone mein apna introduction record karo. Suno. Notice karo kahan 'um', 'err', 'basically' zyada hai. Phir improve karo.
habits.goal: Weekly recording karke improve track karo

habits.description: YouTube par TED Talks ya job interview videos dekho. Jab koi apna introduction deta hai — pause karo, uski exact lines repeat karo. Uski pace, tone, pauses sab copy karo.
Har naye person se milte waqt — chahe auto driver ho, shop owner ho, ya koi bhi — mentally apna English introduction rehearse karo. Imagine kar rahe ho tum interview mein ho.
habits.goal: Keep the introduction always ready in your mind
habits.timeRequired: Mental exercise — no extra time needed

masterSummary.title: The 10 Rules to Never Forget
rules: 1. NEVER say 'Myself Rahul' — always 'My name is Rahul'
2. NEVER say 'My good name' — just 'My name'
3. ALWAYS say 'I WAS born' (not 'I am born') — birth is past
4. NEVER say 'I am working since 3 years' — say 'for 3 years'
5. NEVER say 'I am liking/knowing/having' — use simple present
6. ALWAYS say 'I work AT Google' (not 'in Google') for companies
7. NEVER say 'I am belong to' — say 'I belong to' or 'I am from'
8. ALWAYS check A/AN by the SOUND, not the spelling
9. NEVER say 'passed out from college' — say 'graduated from'
10. ALWAYS end with 'I look forward to' or 'Thank you for this opportunity'
